children.name: Dasypus
children.description: Nine-banded armadillos — the only armadillo with a substantial range in North America and the only armadillo that regularly crosses the USA-Mexico border (it has expanded northward to Nebraska and North Carolina since the 19th century). Nine-banded armadillos are notable for two remarkable biological facts: they always give birth to identical quadruplets (from a single fertilised egg that divides four times), and they are the only non-human animal known to naturally carry Mycobacterium leprae, the bacterium that causes leprosy — human cases in southern USA have been traced to contact with wild armadillos. They are also excellent swimmers, inflating their intestines to become buoyant, or alternatively walking along the bottom of rivers.

children.name: Dasypus novemcinctus
children.description: The only armadillo in North America. Expanding range northward with warming climate. Always gives birth to genetically identical quadruplets. Natural reservoir of leprosy. When startled, leaps straight upward — effective against predators but fatal on roads.

children.name: Dasypus septemcinctus
children.description: A small long-nosed armadillo of central South American savannas, usually showing seven movable bands across its shell.

children.name: Dasypus hybridus
children.description: A grassland armadillo of the Pampas, declining across its range due to agricultural conversion and hunting.

children.name: Dasypus kappleri
children.description: The largest of the long-nosed armadillos, a rainforest species of the Amazon and Guiana Shield with distinctive spurred scales on its hind knees.

children.name: Dasypus pilosus
children.description: A peculiar Peruvian cloud-forest armadillo whose carapace is hidden beneath a dense coat of greyish hair, making it look unlike any other armadillo.

children.name: Dasypus sabanicola
children.description: A small long-nosed armadillo endemic to the Llanos grasslands of Venezuela and adjacent Colombia, living in the seasonally flooded savannas and gallery forests of the Orinoco basin. Closely related to the seven-banded armadillo, it is distinguished by its habitat preference and slightly different morphology. Like other Dasypus species it gives birth to genetically identical polyembryonic litters.

children.name: Dasypus yepesi
children.description: A small armadillo described from the Yungas forests and adjacent lowlands of northwestern Argentina and Bolivia, named in honour of Argentine zoologist Jorge Yepes. Long confused with the southern long-nosed armadillo, it was elevated to full species status on the basis of cranial and dental characters. Inhabits subtropical and tropical moist forests at elevations up to 2,000 metres.

children.name: Tolypeutes
children.description: Three-banded armadillos — the only armadillos able to roll into a complete, sealed ball. Most armadillos protect themselves by digging rapidly into the ground; three-banded armadillos occupy drier, harder-substrate habitats where digging is impractical, and instead evolved a perfectly spherical defensive posture with no gaps. The shell plates fit together so precisely that even a prying human hand struggles to open them. The southern three-banded armadillo is the mascot of the 2014 FIFA World Cup (Fuleco). Both species are native to South America's Cerrado and Caatinga.

children.name: Tolypeutes matacus
children.description: The 2014 World Cup mascot ('Fuleco'). Vulnerable; threatened by habitat loss in the Brazilian Cerrado and Chaco. The only armadillo that can close into a complete sphere with no exposed soft tissue.

children.name: Tolypeutes tricinctus
children.description: An endangered armadillo endemic to the Brazilian Caatinga and Cerrado, also able to roll into a sealed ball; once thought extinct before being rediscovered in the 1990s.

children.name: Priodontes
children.description: The giant armadillo — the world's largest armadillo, reaching 1.5 m in length and weighing up to 60 kg. It has up to 100 small teeth — more than any other land mammal — arranged in rows but used minimally, as it tears apart termite mounds with massive, curved third claws and laps up insects with a long, sticky tongue. The giant armadillo is largely nocturnal and extremely secretive; its population is so sparse that it was barely studied until recent camera-trap surveys. It creates burrows large enough for a human to enter, which are subsequently used as critical shelters for dozens of other species including maned wolves, giant anteaters, tapirs, and pumas.

children.name: Priodontes maximus
children.description: Vulnerable. Has more teeth than any other land mammal (up to 100). Claws up to 20 cm long — the largest of any living animal relative to body size. Keystone species: its burrows shelter dozens of other species.

children.name: Chlamyphorus
children.description: The pink fairy armadillo — the world's smallest armadillo and one of the most bizarre mammals. At just 90–115 mm long, it is the size of a large hamster. Its shell is paper-thin and attached to the spine only by a membrane along the dorsal midline, making it far more flexible than other armadillos. The shell is also pink, reflecting a rich blood supply that allows the animal to thermoregulate by flushing blood through it. The fairy armadillo is a sand-swimmer — it burrows through loose, sandy soil with remarkable speed, essentially swimming underground. Rarely seen and almost impossible to keep in captivity, it remains very poorly known.

children.name: Chlamyphorus truncatus
children.description: The world's smallest armadillo at 90–115 mm. Its pink, translucent shell thermoregulates by flushing with blood. A sand-swimmer of the Argentine pampas. Rarely seen; population unknown. Listed as data deficient.

children.name: Calyptophractus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the greater fairy armadillo, a sand-swimming relative of the pink fairy armadillo from the Gran Chaco.

children.name: Calyptophractus retusus
children.description: A small burrowing armadillo of the Chaco whose rear shield is squared off like a plug to seal its tunnel; squeaks loudly when handled, earning it the name Chacoan fairy armadillo.

children.name: Chaetophractus
children.description: The hairy armadillos — hardy, bristly armadillos of arid and semi-arid South America, among the most abundant and adaptable members of the family.

children.name: Chaetophractus villosus
children.description: A common, coarse-haired armadillo of the Argentine pampas, tolerant of human-altered land and able to survive on a remarkably broad omnivorous diet.

children.name: Chaetophractus vellerosus
children.description: Named for the loud squeals it gives when threatened; a small desert armadillo able to go without drinking by extracting water from its food.

children.name: Chaetophractus nationi
children.description: A high-altitude hairy armadillo found in the puna grasslands and rocky slopes of Bolivia, Peru, and extreme northern Chile, at elevations from 3,000 to over 4,600 metres above sea level. Closely related to the screaming hairy armadillo and adapted to the cold, dry conditions of the Andean altiplano. Its taxonomic status has been debated, but genetic evidence supports its recognition as a distinct species.

children.name: Zaedyus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the pichi, the only armadillo known to undergo true hibernation.

children.name: Zaedyus pichiy
children.description: A small armadillo of Patagonia and the Argentine steppe; when alarmed it wedges itself into the soil and uses the serrated edge of its shell as a shield, and it is unique among armadillos in hibernating through winter.

children.name: Euphractus
children.description: A monotypic genus for the six-banded or yellow armadillo, a stout, diurnal armadillo of central South America.

children.name: Euphractus sexcinctus
children.description: A yellowish, well-armoured armadillo active by day, unusually omnivorous and even scavenging carrion; common across the grasslands and savannas of central South America.

children.name: Cabassous
children.description: The naked-tailed armadillos, powerful diggers whose tails lack the bony armour seen in other armadillos.

children.name: Cabassous unicinctus
children.description: A secretive, fast-burrowing armadillo with a bare, leathery tail and enormous claws used to break into termite and ant nests, its main food.

children.name: Cabassous centralis
children.description: The northernmost naked-tailed armadillo, ranging from southern Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Venezuela. Like all Cabassous species it lacks the bony armour on its tail, and uses powerful, curved forelimbs to excavate burrows and rip apart ant and termite colonies. Rarely observed due to its nocturnal habits and tendency to flee rapidly underground when disturbed.

children.name: Cabassous chacoensis
children.description: The smallest of the naked-tailed armadillos, endemic to the dry Gran Chaco of Paraguay, northwestern Argentina, and adjacent Bolivia. It inhabits thorny scrublands and dry forests, where it digs for ants and termites in hard clay soils. Data Deficient on the IUCN Red List due to limited field surveys; likely threatened by Chaco deforestation, one of the fastest rates of habitat loss in the world.

children.name: Cabassous tatouay
children.description: The largest of the Cabassous species, reaching up to 60 cm, found in the Atlantic Forest and Cerrado of Brazil, Uruguay, Paraguay, and northeastern Argentina. Its common name derives from the Guaraní word for armadillo. Like relatives it feeds principally on ants and termites, excavating nest chambers with powerful forelimbs. Threatened by Atlantic Forest fragmentation and hunting.
